processes.name: business-account-onboarding
inputs: Business formation documents (EIN, articles of incorporation)

steps.name: Application intake
steps.reason: Sales collects the signed application and formation documents upfront so Compliance never has to chase the client for basic paperwork mid-review.

steps.name: Collect formation documents
steps.reason: EIN and articles of incorporation are required before KYB can start — without them Compliance can't verify the business is real.

steps.name: KYB/KYC verification
steps.reason: Required under BSA/AML regulations before any business account can be opened — this is a legal requirement, not a policy choice.

steps.name: Verify beneficial ownership
steps.reason: Regulators require identifying the individuals who ultimately own or control the business before an account can be opened.

steps.name: Screen against OFAC/sanctions lists
steps.reason: Opening an account for a sanctioned entity exposes the company and the partner bank to regulatory penalties.

steps.name: Risk review and approval decision
steps.reason: Higher-risk industries (crypto-adjacent, money services businesses) or flagged screening results require a manual decision beyond what the automated KYB tooling can clear.

steps.name: Treasury account provisioning
steps.reason: The account must be created in the internal ledger and linked to the partner bank before any funds can move — this cannot happen before compliance approval, no exceptions.

steps.name: Platform access and API key issuance
steps.reason: Most startup clients integrate Tabby into their own finance stack, so API credentials need to be issued as soon as the account is live.

steps.name: Customer success handoff
steps.reason: Tribal knowledge gap: the client needs a single ongoing point of contact, but today there is no system trigger for this — Jordan often learns the account is live informally rather than through a defined handoff.

processes.name: feature-launch-coordination
inputs: Approved feature spec and design mockups
outputs: Feature shipped and live in production

steps.name: Feature specification and design handoff
steps.reason: PM translates customer needs and product strategy into a spec that Engineering can build from. Design and PM iterate until Engineering signals readiness to plan.

steps.name: Validate customer need and market fit
steps.reason: Before investing engineering time, confirm this solves a real problem and aligns with roadmap. This prevents wasted engineering cycles on features nobody needs.

steps.name: Create detailed feature spec with success metrics
steps.reason: Specs define what done looks like. Without this, engineering builds to their interpretation and shipping often reveals misalignment.

steps.name: Engineering capacity planning and estimation
steps.reason: Engineering lead estimates effort, identifies dependencies, and commits to timeline. This is the moment the team becomes accountable for shipping.

steps.name: Break feature into tasks and identify tech dependencies
steps.reason: Large features often depend on other in-flight work or infrastructure changes. Missing dependencies late cause delays.

steps.name: Estimate total engineering effort and define milestone dates
steps.reason: Estimation creates the contract between PM and engineering on what ships and when. Vague timelines cause constant scope creep.

steps.name: Security and compliance review
steps.reason: Security team gates features that touch auth, data, or payments. This is non-negotiable but often causes shipping delays if not started early.

steps.name: Development and QA
steps.reason: Engineering executes the spec. QA validates it matches the spec before shipping. This is where most unknowns surface.

steps.name: Launch readiness review and approval decision
steps.reason: PM makes the final call on whether to ship. Shared responsibility with engineering (if something breaks) and product (if it doesn't move the needle).

steps.name: Production deployment and monitoring
steps.reason: Engineering deploys to production and monitors for errors. If something breaks, they roll back or hotfix. PM is on standby to communicate impact.

steps.name: Post-launch customer communication and feedback collection
steps.reason: Customer success lets customers know the feature is live and collects early feedback. This informs whether the feature solved what PM intended.

steps.name: Post-launch metrics and learning
steps.reason: Tribal knowledge gap: most teams never review post-launch metrics or document what went well and what broke. This prevents learning across launches.

Process gaps: Step "Risk review and approval decision" looks like an approval/review checkpoint, but no SLA or turnaround expectation is documented in its reason — what happens when it stalls?

Step "Customer success handoff" admits a gap in its own documented reason: "Tribal knowledge gap: the client needs a single ongoing point of contact, but today there is no system trigger for this — Jordan often learns the account is live informally rather than through a defined handoff."
Documentation gaps: Upstream process "partnership-lead-qualification" is referenced but not documented in ProcessBrain yet.
Downstream process "treasury-cash-management-setup" is referenced but not documented in ProcessBrain yet.

Process gaps: Step "Security and compliance review" looks like an approval/review checkpoint, but no SLA or turnaround expectation is documented in its reason — what happens when it stalls?
Step "Launch readiness review and approval decision" looks like an approval/review checkpoint, but no SLA or turnaround expectation is documented in its reason — what happens when it stalls?

Step "Post-launch metrics and learning" admits a gap in its own documented reason: "Tribal knowledge gap: most teams never review post-launch metrics or document what went well and what broke. This prevents learning across launches."

Overloaded owners: "Priya Anand" owns 7 step(s) across 2 process(es): "Application intake" (business-account-onboarding), "Application intake → Collect formation documents" (business-account-onboarding), "Feature specification and design handoff" (feature-launch-coordination), "Feature specification and design handoff → Validate customer need and market fit" (feature-launch-coordination), "Feature specification and design handoff → Create detailed feature spec with success metrics" (feature-launch-coordination), "Launch readiness review and approval decision" (feature-launch-coordination), "Post-launch metrics and learning" (feature-launch-coordination). Single point of failure risk.

"Marcus Whitfield" owns 5 step(s) across 2 process(es): "KYB/KYC verification" (business-account-onboarding), "KYB/KYC verification → Verify beneficial ownership" (business-account-onboarding), "KYB/KYC verification → Screen against OFAC/sanctions lists" (business-account-onboarding), "Risk review and approval decision" (business-account-onboarding), "Security and compliance review" (feature-launch-coordination). Single point of failure risk.

"Sam Okafor" owns 6 step(s) across 2 process(es): "Platform access and API key issuance" (business-account-onboarding), "Engineering capacity planning and estimation" (feature-launch-coordination), "Engineering capacity planning and estimation → Break feature into tasks and identify tech dependencies" (feature-launch-coordination), "Engineering capacity planning and estimation → Estimate total engineering effort and define milestone dates" (feature-launch-coordination), "Development and QA" (feature-launch-coordination), "Production deployment and monitoring" (feature-launch-coordination). Single point of failure risk.

insights.text: Compliance's risk review step has no defined SLA, and per leadership notes flagged applications have sat for up to a week with no escalation trigger — this is likely the single biggest drag on time-to-first-deposit, which is a stated company priority. Suggested next step: define a 48-hour SLA for flagged reviews with an automatic escalation to Marcus if unresolved, then track actual review time to confirm impact.
